data.title: Europe's fallback plan if U.S. exits NATO is gaining traction - report
data.description:
A contingency plan to ensure Europe's defense using NATO's existing military structures is gaining traction after getting buy-in from Germany, The Wall Street Journal reported, as U.S. President Trump threatens to exit the alliance.

Officials working on the plan are seeking to place more Europeans in NATO's command-and-control roles and supplement U.S. military assets with their own.

The plan is aimed at preserving deterrence against Russia, operational continuity and nuclear credibility even if the U.S. pulls its forces from Europe or refuses to support the region's defense.

Germany has long resisted calls for greater European sovereignty in its defense, but its stance shifted in recent months due to concerns over the dependability of the U.S. as an ally under Trump.

Berlin's shift led to a broader agreement among other countries – including the U.K., France, Poland, the Nordic countries and Canada – which are casting the contingency plan as a coalition-of-the-willing within NATO, according to officials involved.

"A burden shifting from the U.S. toward Europe is ongoing and it will continue," Finnish President Alexander Stubb, one of the leaders involved in the contingency planning, told WSJ. He added that this shift is being done "in a very managed and controllable way."

data.title: eToro to acquire Zengo; bolsters self-custodial and on-chain infrastructure
Biggest stock movers Wednesday: GTLB, AVGO, and more
data.description:
Stock futures stayed cautious Wednesday morning as traders watched for potential new U.S.-Iran peace talks this week while tracking a heavy slate of corporate earnings reports.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • GitLab (GTLB) +7% - Shares surged after the company expanded its partnership with Google Cloud, enabling customers to integrate GitLab’s Duo Agent Platform with Vertex AI models, with usage counting toward existing cloud commitments. The collaboration strengthens GitLab’s positioning in AI-driven software development by combining its end-to-end DevSecOps platform with advanced AI capabilities, enhancing its role in the evolving agent-based development ecosystem.
  • Broadcom (AVGO) +3% - Shares rose after announcing a multi-year AI partnership with Meta Platforms ((META)) to develop and deploy custom MTIA chips across data centers, with an initial commitment exceeding 1 GW and a broader multi-generation rollout planned through 2029. The deal reinforces Broadcom’s positioning in AI infrastructure and custom silicon while deepening its role in Meta’s long-term compute strategy.
  • Stellantis (STLA) +3% - Shares gained after reporting an estimated ~12% Y/Y increase in Q1 global shipments to 1.36M units, driven by broad-based strength across regions. North America led with a 17% gain on strong demand for key models, while Europe, Asia Pacific, and other regions also posted solid growth supported by new vehicle launches and platform-driven momentum, signaling resilient demand across its global portfolio.

Biggest stock losers

  • TeraWulf (WULF) -5% - Shares fell after pricing an upsized $900M equity offering, raising dilution concerns, alongside softer-than-expected preliminary Q1 results with revenue of $30M–$35M below consensus and adjusted EBITDA to be between $0M and $3M. The company said proceeds will fund data center expansion, debt repayment, and new site development, but the combination of capital raise and weak near-term performance weighed on sentiment.

data.title: Europe markets muted over Iran de-escalation hopes; luxury stocks slump
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.02% to 10,606. 

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.01% to 24,047. 

France (CAC:IND) -0.80% to 8,261. France inflation rises to 1.7% in March, meeting estimates.

In other parts of Europe, the annual inflation rate in Poland rose to 3% in March.

Slovakia’s annual inflation rate eased to 3.5% in March.

Norway’s trade surplus widened to NOK 97.5B in March.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) traded 0.04% lower to 619.6,  as investors awaited further developments in the Middle East and assessed a fresh batch of corporate earnings from major companies. Luxury stocks fell to the bottom of Stoxx 600 after Hermes (HESAY) and Kering (PPRUY) reported Q1 sales missed expectations as the Iran conflict hit Middle East demand and tourism in Europe. Both stocks dropped sharply after weak Q1 updates, with Hermès (HESAF) hit by macro/geopolitics and Kering (PPRUF) by Gucci-specific weakness, dragging the entire European luxury sector lower.

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury was almost flat at 4.26%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 1 basis point to 4.78%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was down 1 basis point to 3.02%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Hermes International reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Hermes International press release (HESAY): Q1 consolidated revenue amounted to €4.07B in the first quarter of 2026, up 6% at constant exchange rates compared with 2025.
  • Outlook : "In the medium-term, despite the economic, geopolitical and monetary uncertainties around the world, the group confirms an ambitious goal for revenue growth at constant exchange rates.
  • In a still uncertain economic and geopolitical context, the group has moved into 2026 with confidence, thanks to its highly integrated artisanal model, balanced distribution network, the creativity of collections and the loyalty of clients."

data.title: Kering SA reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Kering SA press release (PPRUF): Q1 Revenue of €3.57B (-6.3% Y/Y).
  • Outlook: "In a still uncertain geopolitical and macroeconomic environment, the Group places a strong emphasis on agility and flawless execution, equipping each House with sharper, more sustainable brand strategies and the operational support required to accelerate progress. As Kering progresses through 2026, its objective remains to return to growth and improve margins. The Capital Markets Day on April 16, 2026, will present in detail the roadmap that will guide this next phase in the transformation of Kering."

data.title: BlackBerry's QNX partners with TKMS to support Canada’s next-gen submarine program
Stock futures cautious after rally with more earnings on tap
data.description:
Stock index futures were slightly higher before the bell Tuesday as investors digested news that potential talks with Iran could begin within two days, lending credibility to diplomatic developments and fueling an optimistic tone.

Investors will also parse more bank/financial earnings.

Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (INDU) rose +0.11%, while S&P 500 futures (SPX) edged up +0.05%. Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) were essentially flat, ticking higher by +0.003%.

“Investors have received enough independent confirmation to give the idea credibility,” UBS Chief Economist Paul Donovan said of the potential Iran talks. “The ‘always look on the bright side’ mentality has continued.”

Treasury yields rose across the curve during premarket trading. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) climbed to 4.26%, up 0.19%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved to 3.77%, gaining 0.29%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) inched up to 4.87%.

Analysts have noted mounting evidence that markets are building a risk premium at the long end of the yield curve.

Top gainers in premarket trading included Robinhood Markets (HOOD) +4.67%, Broadcom (AVGO) +3.21%, and Amcor (AMCR) +2.61%. Decliners included Regency Centers (REG) -6.46%, Snap-on (SNA) -2.71%, and Veralto (VLTO) -2.59%.

data.title: Ares CEO says no signs of major default cycle in private credit
data.description:
Private credit defaults are relatively contained, with most of the stress in the sector being ​liquidity- and rate-driven, the head of Ares Management Corp. (ARES) ‌said on Wednesday.

"There's nothing that we're seeing in our portfolios or in the market broadly that would say that we're about to ​have a major default cycle," Michael Arougheti, chief executive ​and co-founder of the alternative investment firm, ⁠said at the HSBC Investment Summit in Hong Kong.

The private credit industry has recently come under pressure from record redemption requests and growing concerns over valuations, liquidity, and transparency. Investors reportedly sought to withdraw more than $20B of their investments from private credit firms in the first quarter of 2026, highlighting rising unease.

Wall Street executives said they are stress-testing and closely monitoring private credit portfolios, though they remain comfortable with their exposure.

Much of the recent pressure has been tied to a repricing in the software sector, said Rachel Lord, a senior executive at BlackRock (BLK), speaking at the same forum.

“There's ​volatility, but there isn't a bubble,” Lord said.

“The fog of private credit is ​going to clear.”
